summary:
The Habsburg Empire (c. 1500–1806 AD) was the dominant dynastic power of early modern Europe — a multinational aggregation of territories assembled through strategic marriage ('Bella gerant alii, tu felix Austria nube': 'Let others wage war; you, happy Austria, marry') that at its peak under [[event:2497|Charles V]] encompassed Spain, the Spanish Americas, the Holy Roman Empire, the Netherlands, southern Italy, and Austria, creating the first empire on which 'the sun never set.' The Habsburgs' power rested not on ethnic or linguistic unity (their subjects spoke German, Spanish, Italian, Czech, Hungarian, Dutch, and dozens of other languages) but on dynastic legitimacy, Catholic faith, and administrative efficiency — a pre-national form of political organization that would eventually prove incompatible with the nationalism of the 19th century.

The Habsburg Empire's history after Charles V divided between the Spanish and Austrian branches. The Spanish Habsburgs ruled until 1700, when the last Spanish Habsburg died without an heir, triggering the [[event:2347|War of the Spanish Succession]]. The Austrian Habsburgs continued in Vienna, ruling the Holy Roman Empire (until Napoleon abolished it in 1806) and then the Austrian Empire. The Habsburg 'composite monarchy' served as both the champion of Counter-Reformation Catholicism (driving the [[event:2328|Thirty Years' War]]) and as the bulwark against Ottoman expansion, defending Vienna in 1529 and 1683. The empire's ultimate failure to create a coherent national identity from its diverse peoples made it increasingly vulnerable in an age of nationalism, and it would only finally collapse under the pressures of World War I in 1918 — though it left enduring cultural legacies across Central Europe.
